# RFC-0001: Benchmark execution and assessment model

> **Version:** 0.1.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** stable

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0001:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0001c-summary"></a>

This RFC defines the execution and assessment model for `agent-skill-bench`.

**Scope:** benchmark suites and cases, case resolution, runtime execution, deterministic assessment, optional judge assessment, and normalized run artifacts.

**Rationale:** the framework needs a stable behavioral model that is independent of any single benchmark suite or runtime adapter and can be verified consistently across implementations.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0001:C-SUITE-AND-CASE-RESOLUTION] Suite and case resolution (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-suite-and-case-resolution"></a>

A benchmark execution MUST resolve one suite definition and one case definition into a single resolved case before invoking any runtime.
The suite MUST define the benchmark-wide defaults that the framework needs for execution and assessment, including execution defaults and any prompt or assessment contracts that apply to the suite.
A case MAY override suite defaults where the framework explicitly permits overrides.
The resolved case MUST preserve the selected suite ID, case ID, mode, execution kind, source path, and the effective policies that will govern execution and assessment.

**Rationale:** benchmark runs need a reproducible pre-execution representation so runtime behavior and later assessment can be explained against a single effective configuration.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-SUITE-OWNED-SEMANTICS] Suite-owned semantics (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-suite-owned-semantics"></a>

The shared benchmark framework MUST interpret suite-owned contracts and policies generically rather than embedding benchmark-domain semantics in framework code.
A suite MUST be able to define its own prompt contract, deterministic rules, and judge policy without requiring the framework to hardcode domain-specific section names, anti-pattern phrases, or rubric language.
A runtime adapter MUST NOT become the authority for suite semantics.

**Rationale:** the framework is shared across skill domains, while the meaning of benchmark expectations belongs to the suite that defines them.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-RUNTIME-EXECUTION] Runtime execution model (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-runtime-execution"></a>

Candidate execution MUST compile a resolved case into a runtime request and execute it through a shared agent-runtime abstraction.
Judge execution MUST compile a judge task into the same shared agent-runtime abstraction rather than inventing a separate vendor-specific execution model.
A runtime adapter MUST return the candidate or judge output text.
When a runtime request includes structured-output constraints, the runtime adapter MUST either return parsed structured output that satisfies those constraints or fail the run.
The runtime abstraction MUST be capable of carrying execution intent such as purpose, working directory controls, skill bindings, timeout controls, and optional structured-output constraints.

**Rationale:** candidate and judge runs differ in purpose, but they share the same class of runtime behavior and should be modeled uniformly. Structured judge execution is not optional once the request asks for validated structured output.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-ASSESSMENT-AND-ARTIFACTS] Assessment and run artifacts (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-assessment-and-artifacts"></a>

Each benchmark run MUST produce a deterministic rule assessment, even when no judge runtime is configured.
A benchmark run MAY produce a judge assessment when a judge runtime is configured.
A normalized run artifact MUST include enough information to identify the executed case, the candidate runtime used, the candidate output, deterministic assessment results, and any judge assessment that was produced.
When deterministic checks fail, the artifact MUST preserve stable failure-mode codes derived from the failed checks.

**Rationale:** regression tracking depends on a persistent, comparable artifact that records both the generated output and the framework's interpretation of the run.

*Since: v0.1.0*
